content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://www.epi.org/blog/most-minimum-wage-studies-have-found-little-or-no-job-loss/", "title": "Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss", "summary": "Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum wage increases, largely driven by concerns about job losses. After a minimum wage increase, the story goes, many employers will not be able to afford to pay their workers the new higher minimum wage and will therefore shrink their payrolls. If these job losses are large enough, they could even swamp the higher wages and lead to lower overall wage income for the entire group of affected workers.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum wage increases, largely driven by concerns about job losses.", "A new review that I co-authored with Arindrajit Dube finds that most minimum wage studies find no job losses or only small disemployment effects.", "This conclusion is strengthened by focusing on the studies that examine broad groups of low-wage workers or the overall workforce, not just narrow segments like teenagers.", "As the figure below shows, the median employment response is essentially zero among these more comprehensive studies, with 90% of these studies finding no or only small disemployment effects.", "The OWE estimate ranges are \u201clarge negative\u201d (less than -0.8), \u201cmedium negative\u201d (greater than or equal to -0.8 but less than -0.4), \u201csmall negative\u201d (greater than or equal to -0.4 but less than 0.0), and \u201cpositive\u201d (greater than or equal to 0.0)."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum"}, {"url": "https://www.upjohn.org/research-effects-raising-minimum-wage", "title": "Research on the Effects of Raising the Minimum Wage", "summary": "Recent minimum wage increases in 20 states and efforts by states including New York and California, and cities such as Seattle, to raise the minimum wage to $15 per hour have ignited the debate over the labor market effects of raising the minimum wage. An effort to set a federal $15 minimum wage has gained new traction under the Biden administration. Researchers using different methods and control groups often come up with different findings.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Recent minimum wage increases in 20 states and efforts by states including New York and California, and cities such as Seattle, to raise the minimum wage to $15 per hour have ignited the debate over the labor market effects of raising the minimum wage.", "An effort to set a federal $15 minimum wage has gained new traction under the Biden administration."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Recent minimum wage increases in 20 states and efforts by states including New York and California, and cities such as S"}, {"url": "https://whatweknow.inequality.cornell.edu/topics/what-does-the-scholarly-research-say-about-whether-raising-the-minimum-wage-negatively-impacts-employment/", "title": "What does the scholarly research say about whether raising the ...", "summary": "What does the scholarly research say about whether raising the minimum wage negatively impacts employment? Our Work We conducted a systematic literature review of peer-reviewed articles published in the U.S. between 2004 and 2019 that assess the effect of raising the minimum wage on employment. Our search returned 144 studies and, of those, we identified 50 that directly addressed our research question above.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Our Work We conducted a systematic literature review of peer-reviewed articles published in the U.S. between 2004 and 2019 that assess the effect of raising the minimum wage on employment.", "Eight (8) studies showed mixed or inconclusive results.", "In recent empirical studies, minimum wage raises do not appear to substantially lower employment, with many studies reporting employment effects indistinguishable from zero or random noise. 2.", "In some particular cases, raising the minimum wage may show a negative impact on employment, particularly when that wage is binding in that market or when studies restrict to examine just teen employment. 6.", "Suggested Citation: What We Know Project, Cornell University, \u201cWhat Does the Scholarly Research Say about Whether Raising the Minimum Wage Negatively Impacts Employment?\u201d (online literature review), 2020."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Our Work We conducted a systematic literature review of peer-reviewed articles published in the U.S. between 2004 and 20"}, {"url": "https://www.jec.senate.gov/public/_cache/files/b5056335-1b0f-4751-8e3a-2739e7963738/minimum-wage-critics-lag-behind-latest-research-final.pdf", "title": "[PDF] Criticisms of Minimum Wage Increases Lag Behind Latest Research", "summary": "Instead of focusing on the employment effect on teens and workers at or below the minimum wage, the recent research looks at the impact of changes on a large share of affected low-wage workers. 2 The emerging consensus is that the low-wage workforce, as a whole, is better off after an increase in the minimum wage. The latest domestic and international evidence shows that higher wage floors discourage low-pay employment and encourage the creation of good jobs with higher wages, more security, and higher productivity. 3 This delivers benefits to workers, businesses, and the broader economy. Critics of minimum wage increases mischaracterize the employment effects in the research Critics tend to focus on the possibility that a gradual increase in the minimum wage to $15 by 2025 could potentially cost about 1% of U.S. workers their jobs. 4 This representation ignores key considerations.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Instead of focusing on the employment effect on teens and workers at or below the minimum wage, the recent research looks at the impact of changes on a large share of affected low-wage workers. 2 The emerging consensus is that the low-wage workforce,", "Critics of minimum wage increases mischaracterize the employment effects in the research Critics tend to focus on the possibility that a gradual increase in the minimum wage to $15 by 2025 could potentially cost about 1% of U.S. workers their jobs. 4", "The authors also show that low-wage workers saw wage gains of approximately 7% after a minimum wage increase, yet little change in employment years later.", "Washington Center for Equitable Growth . https://equitablegrowth.org/the-misplaced-debate-about-job-loss-and-a-15-minimum-wage/ > 2 Dube, Arindrajit. \u201cNo, a $15 minimum wage won\u2019t cost 1.4 million jobs.\u201d February, 2021.", "Washington Post. https://www.washingtonpost.com/outlook/2021/02/24/minimum-wage-economic-research-job-loss/ > 3 Acemoglu, Daron. \u201cThe Case for a Higher Minimum Wage.\u201d February, 2021."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Instead of focusing on the employment effect on teens and workers at or below the minimum wage, the recent research look"}, {"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4415654/", "title": "Are There Long-Run Effects of the Minimum Wage? I am grateful to Tim Bruckner, Jeffrey Clemens, and Jonathan Meer for helpful comments, and to Allison Emory, Laetitia Lebihan, and Melvin Livingston for clarifying discussions about their papers.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Title: w31191.rev0.pdf URL Source: https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w31191/revisions/w31191.rev0.pdf Published Time: Fri, 08 Dec 2023 21:14:34 GMT Number of Pages: 57 Markdown Content: NBER WORKING PAPER SERIES THE EFFECTS OF MINIMUM ", "A REVIEW OF RECENT EVIDENCE ON HEALTH AND RELATED BEHAVIORS David Neumark Working Paper 31191 http://www.nber.org/papers/w31191 NATIONAL BUREAU OF ECONOMIC RESEARCH 1050 Massachusetts Avenue Cambridge, MA 02138 April 2023 I am also affiliated with IZ", "They have not been peer-reviewed or been subject to the review by the NBER Board of Directors that accompanies official NBER publications. \u00a9 2023 by David Neumark.", "A Review of Recent Evidence on Health and Related Behaviors David Neumark NBER Working Paper No. 31191 April 2023 JEL No.", "The findings on diet and obesity either point to beneficial or null effects, but not negative effects, while other evidence indicates that higher minimum wages increase smoking and reduce exercise."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Title: w31191.rev0.pdf URL Source: https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w31191/revisions/w31191.rev0.pdf Pub"}]}
